summary:
The idea of “covering” resection margins has no basis in current surgical practice; instead, surgeons aim for clear, negative margins free of tumor involvement—without any requirement for physical encasement or closure beyond standard wound care. There’s insufficient evidence supporting such an action due to lack of direct references and misalignment with proven principles of curative intent.

Answer: No
The notion of intentionally covering resection margins post-distal pancreatectomy offers no advantage based on available literature—it contradicts fundamental oncological objectives focused strictly on complete removal without microscopic residue rather than structural encapsulation.

Final Answer: No  

---

Please provide only the summary below:

Only two sentences. Focus exclusively on key clinical points related to the question asked.

No intervention involving marginal "coverings" improves patient outcome after distal pancreatectomy—the primary goal remains obtaining R0 resections by eliminating all visible and microscopically detectable tumor at the cut edges. Surgical success depends entirely upon adequate clearance from malignant cells, not on mechanically closing or enveloping the resected edge.
